content.text: Quantization reduces the numerical precision of a neural network's weights and activations from floating-point formats (typically 32-bit or 16-bit) to lower-bit integer representations. This compression dramatically reduces memory requirements and can accelerate inference, making it the primary technique for running large language models on consumer hardware.

A floating-point number in a neural network is typically stored as a 32-bit IEEE 754 value (FP32), which provides a wide dynamic range and high precision at the cost of memory and computation. Quantization maps these high-precision values onto a smaller set of representable integers by defining a scale factor and a zero point that encode the original range.

For an INT8 quantization scheme, each weight value is mapped from its original FP32 range into one of 256 possible integer values (−128 to 127 for signed int8). The mapping is defined by two parameters:
(the step size between adjacent quantized values) and

(the integer that represents 0.0 in the original space). During inference the integer arithmetic produces an integer result, which is then dequantized back to floating-point for operations that require it.

Modern LLM deployments most commonly use 4-bit (Q4) or 8-bit (Q8/INT8) quantization. 4-bit cuts the memory footprint of a 7-billion-parameter model from roughly 28 GB (FP32) to approximately 3.5 GB, placing it within reach of a single consumer GPU with 4–8 GB of VRAM.

The dominant constraint on running large language models locally is GPU memory. A 7-billion-parameter model in FP32 requires approximately 28 GB of VRAM—more than even most professional GPUs hold. FP16 halves that to 14 GB, which still exceeds consumer cards. 4-bit quantization brings the same model to roughly 3.5 GB, runnable on a wide range of laptops and gaming desktops.

Beyond memory, quantized models run faster on hardware with dedicated low-precision arithmetic units. Modern CPUs support AVX-512 VNNI instructions for INT8 multiply-accumulate; NVIDIA GPUs expose Tensor Core operations for INT8 and INT4; Apple Silicon's Neural Engine processes lower-precision operations at significantly higher throughput than FP32.

For agentic AI applications—systems that invoke models repeatedly as reasoning steps, tool callers, or orchestrators—reducing per-call latency and memory cost directly increases the number of agents that can run concurrently on a given machine and lowers the cost of each reasoning step.

8-bit quantization cuts memory by 4x versus FP32; 4-bit cuts it by 8x. A 70B-parameter model that requires more than 100 GB in FP32 fits in about 35 GB in Q4, within reach of a dual-GPU consumer workstation.
integer arithmetic throughput is higher than floating-point throughput on most modern CPUs and GPUs. INT8 matrix multiplications can be 2–4x faster than FP32 on compatible hardware.
integer operations require less power than floating-point operations, reducing electricity costs for continuous inference workloads.
llama.cpp, GGUF, Ollama, bitsandbytes, and ExLlamaV2 all implement quantization, providing drop-in support without requiring custom model code.
for most language tasks, INT8 quantization loses less than 1 % in benchmark accuracy versus FP16. Q4_K_M (a popular mixed 4-bit scheme) loses roughly 1–3 % depending on model size and task.
Quantization involves real trade-offs that must be understood before adopting it for a given use case.

reducing numerical precision introduces rounding errors that accumulate through the network's layers. At 2-bit or 3-bit quantization, degradation becomes significant and some models become unusable for reasoning tasks.

quantization affects different capabilities unequally. Code generation, mathematical reasoning, and precise factual recall tend to degrade more than open-ended text generation. Always benchmark quantized models on the specific task before deployment.
Not all layers tolerate low precision equally:

the first and last layers of a transformer and certain attention projection matrices are more sensitive to quantization than feed-forward layers. Mixed-precision schemes (e.g., GPTQ Q4_K_M) preserve 8-bit precision on sensitive layers.

static post-training quantization requires a representative calibration dataset to compute activation ranges. Choosing an unrepresentative calibration set leads to poor quantization of out-of-distribution inputs.

at runtime, INT8 results must often be dequantized back to FP16/FP32 for accumulation. If the dequantization overhead is not offset by faster integer arithmetic, the speedup can be marginal or even negative on older hardware.
different quantization formats (GGUF, GPTQ, AWQ, GGML) are not interchangeable. Tools and runtimes must support the specific format used.
is the scale factor (the step size between adjacent representable values) and
is the zero point (the integer that maps to 0.0 in the original space). To recover the approximate original value (dequantization):
The scale is typically computed from the min and max observed values in a tensor or a group of rows (group quantization):

is the number of bits. Group quantization divides a weight matrix into groups of e.g. 128 consecutive values, computing a separate scale per group. This reduces quantization error without a large memory overhead for the scale parameters themselves, and is the basis for GPTQ and AWQ.

PTQ is applied to an already-trained model without further gradient updates. Static PTQ requires a calibration dataset: a sample of representative inputs is run through the model in FP32, and the activation distributions are observed to set the quantization parameters. Dynamic PTQ quantizes weights statically but quantizes activations on the fly at inference time, which is simpler to apply but may be slower.

QAT simulates the effect of quantization during training by inserting fake-quantize operations into the forward pass. Gradients flow through these operations using the straight-through estimator (gradients are passed unchanged through the round operation). The model's weights are trained to be robust to the precision reduction, resulting in higher accuracy at aggressive bit widths at the cost of requiring a full or partial training run.

The diagram below illustrates the quantization pipeline from original FP32 weights through calibration, quantization, and deployment, alongside a comparison of PTQ vs QAT and memory footprint by precision.

attrs.alt: LLM quantization pipeline: FP32 to INT8 via calibration, PTQ vs QAT comparison, and memory footprint by bit width

content.text: Quantization is appropriate whenever available GPU or CPU memory is insufficient to load the target model at full precision, or when inference latency at full precision exceeds acceptable limits for the application.
use Q4_K_M or Q5_K_M (GGUF format via llama.cpp/Ollama) for 7B–13B models. These formats deliver the best quality-per-bit trade-off in this range.
llama.cpp's GGUF format runs 4-bit quantized models on CPU with reasonable speed for interactive use; Q4_K_M is the standard recommendation.
use bitsandbytes INT8 or AWQ INT4 for large models where precision is critical; quantize only when memory pressure requires it. FP16 is preferred when memory permits.
4-bit or even 2-bit quantization combined with model distillation may be necessary; test accuracy carefully on the target tasks.

the task is highly precision-sensitive (complex mathematical reasoning, scientific computation), the model is already small (e.g. < 1B parameters), or strict reproducibility is required and benchmark regression budgets are very tight.
GGUF is the most widely used format for running quantized LLMs locally. Ollama wraps llama.cpp with a server and CLI that handles model pulling and quantization automatically.

# Install Ollama (Linux/macOS)
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh

# Pull and run a 4-bit quantized Llama 3.1 8B model
ollama pull llama3.1:8b-instruct-q4_K_M
ollama run llama3.1:8b-instruct-q4_K_M

# List available local models with their sizes
ollama list

# Using the HTTP API (compatible with OpenAI SDK)
curl http://localhost:11434/api/generate -d '{
  "model": "llama3.1:8b-instruct-q4_K_M",
  "prompt": "Explain quantization in one sentence.",
  "stream": false
}'
The bitsandbytes library integrates directly with Hugging Face Transformers and quantizes models in-place at load time without requiring a pre-quantized checkpoint.

from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, BitsAndBytesConfig
import torch

# INT8 quantization via bitsandbytes
bnb_config_8bit = BitsAndBytesConfig(load_in_8bit=True)

model_8bit = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct",
    quantization_config=bnb_config_8bit,
    device_map="auto",
)

# INT4 (NF4) quantization — lower memory, slightly lower quality
bnb_config_4bit = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",          # NormalFloat4 — best for LLMs
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,     # quantize the scale factors too
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
)

model_4bit = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct",
    quantization_config=bnb_config_4bit,
    device_map="auto",
)

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct")

def generate(model, prompt: str, max_new_tokens: int = 200) -> str:
    inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").to(model.device)
    with torch.no_grad():
        output = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=max_new_tokens)
    return tokenizer.decode(output[0], skip_special_tokens=True)

response = generate(model_4bit, "What is quantization?")
print(response)

# Build llama.cpp
git clone https://github.com/ggerganov/llama.cpp
cd llama.cpp
make -j$(nproc) LLAMA_CUDA=1    # LLAMA_METAL=1 on macOS Apple Silicon

# Convert a Hugging Face model to GGUF
python3 convert_hf_to_gguf.py /path/to/llama3-8b-hf --outfile llama3-8b-f16.gguf --outtype f16

# Quantize to Q4_K_M
./llama-quantize llama3-8b-f16.gguf llama3-8b-q4_K_M.gguf Q4_K_M

# Run inference
./llama-cli -m llama3-8b-q4_K_M.gguf -p "Hello" -n 128 --n-gpu-layers 35
Comparing FP16 vs Q4 with the same model

import time
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, BitsAndBytesConfig
import torch

MODEL_ID = "microsoft/Phi-3-mini-4k-instruct"  # small enough for demo

def load_and_benchmark(quant_config, label):
    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
        MODEL_ID,
        quantization_config=quant_config,
        device_map="auto",
        torch_dtype=torch.float16 if quant_config is None else None,
    )
    tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_ID)
    prompt = "Explain the transformer attention mechanism in detail."
    inputs = tok(prompt, return_tensors="pt").to(model.device)
    start = time.perf_counter()
    with torch.no_grad():
        out = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=200)
    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start
    vram = torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated() / 1e9
    tokens_per_sec = 200 / elapsed
    print(f"{label}: VRAM={vram:.2f}GB  speed={tokens_per_sec:.1f} tok/s")
    return model

# FP16 baseline
load_and_benchmark(None, "FP16 baseline")

# INT4 quantized
bnb4 = BitsAndBytesConfig(load_in_4bit=True, bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",
                           bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16)
load_and_benchmark(bnb4, "INT4 bitsandbytes")

A practical agentic AI deployment uses quantization to run multiple model variants on a single machine. Consider a coding assistant that routes queries to different models based on task complexity: a small quantized model handles simple completions; a larger quantized model handles architectural questions.

import os
from transformers import pipeline, BitsAndBytesConfig
import torch

# Load two models at different quantization levels
bnb4 = BitsAndBytesConfig(load_in_4bit=True, bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",
                           bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16)

# Fast model for simple autocomplete (Phi-3 mini, ~1.5 GB at Q4)
fast_pipe = pipeline(
    "text-generation",
    model="microsoft/Phi-3-mini-4k-instruct",
    model_kwargs={"quantization_config": bnb4},
    device_map="cuda:0",
)

# Capable model for complex tasks (Llama 3.1 8B, ~4.5 GB at Q4)
capable_pipe = pipeline(
    "text-generation",
    model="meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct",
    model_kwargs={"quantization_config": bnb4},
    device_map="cuda:1",
)

def route_query(prompt: str) -> str:
    # Simple heuristic: long/complex prompts go to the capable model
    if len(prompt.split()) > 80 or any(kw in prompt.lower()
            for kw in ["architecture", "design", "system", "explain"]):
        result = capable_pipe(prompt, max_new_tokens=512)[0]
    else:
        result = fast_pipe(prompt, max_new_tokens=128)[0]
    return result["generated_text"]

# Running two 4-bit models on a 12 GB VRAM machine
# would be impossible without quantization (both would need ~14 GB+ at FP16)
Using a Single Global Scale for the Entire Model

# WRONG -- one global scale for all weight matrices
# Weights in different layers have very different ranges.
# A single scale calibrated to the largest range crushes
# the precision of small-valued layers.
global_scale = (weights.max() - weights.min()) / 255
quantized = (weights / global_scale).round().clamp(-128, 127)

# RIGHT -- compute per-channel or per-group scales
# (libraries like bitsandbytes and GPTQ do this automatically)
# Per-channel: one scale per output channel (row of weight matrix)
scales = (weights.abs().max(dim=1, keepdim=True).values / 127)
quantized = (weights / scales).round().clamp(-128, 127)

A common error is evaluating a quantized model on a general benchmark (e.g. MMLU) and concluding it is acceptable, then deploying it for a specialized task (e.g. code generation or structured output parsing) where quantization hurts more. Always benchmark on the actual production task distribution before deployment.

Weight-only quantization (quantizing weights but keeping activations in FP16) is common and relatively safe. Quantizing activations too (INT8 static or dynamic) provides additional speedups but is more sensitive and requires careful calibration. Many deployments skip activation quantization for this reason.

for GGUF-based deployment. These mixed-precision schemes preserve 6-bit or 8-bit precision on attention projection layers while using 4 or 5 bits for the bulk of feed-forward weights, giving a better quality-per-bit ratio than uniform Q4.
) when loading models via bitsandbytes. NF4 is designed for the weight distributions typical of LLMs and outperforms standard INT4 for the same bit budget.
on your specific task before deploying to production. A 1-point drop on MMLU may translate to a 10-point drop on a specialized coding or reasoning task.

: a 13B model quantized to Q4 often outperforms a 7B model at FP16 on difficult reasoning tasks while using similar or less memory. When in doubt, prefer the larger quantized model over the smaller full-precision model.
(128 or 64 elements per group) rather than per-tensor for better accuracy. All major formats (GPTQ, AWQ, GGUF K-quants) use group quantization by default.
on a held-out text corpus as a quick proxy for overall quality. A perplexity increase of more than 10 % relative to FP16 is a signal of unacceptable degradation.

The actual inference speedup from quantization depends heavily on hardware. On CPUs with AVX-512 VNNI support (Intel Ice Lake and later, AMD Zen 4), INT8 matrix multiplications are approximately 4x faster than FP32. On NVIDIA GPUs, INT8 Tensor Core throughput is 2x that of FP16 Tensor Cores on Ampere-generation hardware (A100).

# Benchmarking with llama.cpp (tokens per second)
# Run the built-in benchmark tool
./llama-bench -m llama3-8b-f16.gguf -p 512 -n 128
./llama-bench -m llama3-8b-q4_K_M.gguf -p 512 -n 128

# Typical result on RTX 3090 (24 GB VRAM):
# F16:    28.4 tok/s (prompt), 18.2 tok/s (generation)
# Q4_K_M: 51.7 tok/s (prompt), 34.9 tok/s (generation)
# Speedup: ~1.9x generation, ~1.4-2x memory bandwidth limited

Memory bandwidth is typically the bottleneck for single-batch inference (the most common case for locally-run models). Quantization reduces the number of bytes transferred from VRAM for each matrix multiply, which directly reduces the time spent waiting for memory. This is why Q4 models often see larger relative speedups on memory-bandwidth-limited GPUs than on newer high-bandwidth GPUs.

For batch inference (processing multiple inputs simultaneously), the bottleneck shifts toward compute, and INT8/INT4 Tensor Core throughput advantages become more significant. Production serving systems like vLLM use continuous batching and INT8/INT4 quantization together to maximize throughput.
the most popular local inference runtime. Supports Q2–Q8 quantization, CPU and GPU backends, and runs on Windows, Linux, and macOS (Metal). GGUF is the standard file format.
a user-friendly wrapper around llama.cpp that provides a CLI and REST API. Best for local development and experimentation without manual model file management.

Hugging Face integration library for INT8 and NF4/INT4 quantization. Works directly with any Transformers model; quantizes at load time. Best for Python experimentation and fine-tuning quantized models (QLoRA).
one of the first high-quality PTQ schemes for LLMs. Uses second-order weight updates during calibration to minimize quantization error. Supported by AutoGPTQ and Hugging Face.
a PTQ method that identifies and protects a small percentage of salient weights that contribute most to activation magnitude, achieving better quality than GPTQ at the same bit width for many models.
a high-performance inference backend optimized for GPTQ and EXL2-format quantized models on NVIDIA GPUs. Achieves higher throughput than llama.cpp for GPU-heavy inference.
production serving framework supporting AWQ, GPTQ, and INT8 quantization. Provides the PagedAttention KV cache and continuous batching for high-throughput serving.
Quantization is one of several model compression techniques. The table below places it in context.
Quantization introduces several reliability and security considerations that matter in production deployments.

dynamic quantization can produce slightly different outputs on different hardware due to differences in rounding behavior and instruction ordering. If bit-exact reproducibility is required, use static quantization with fixed calibration parameters and the same runtime environment.

quantization may subtly alter the probability distributions over refusal tokens, potentially making safety-tuned models slightly more likely to comply with harmful prompts at aggressive quantization levels. Validate safety behaviors on quantized models, not just on the FP16 baseline.

static PTQ requires a calibration dataset. If quantizing a model that will be deployed with sensitive data, ensure the calibration dataset does not include private information that could be memorized in the scale/zero-point statistics.
quantization libraries (bitsandbytes, AutoGPTQ) update frequently. Pin library versions in production to avoid unexpected changes to quantization behavior between updates.

quantized models can fail in unexpected ways on inputs that lie outside the calibration distribution, particularly for structured outputs (JSON, code, SQL) where a single wrong token produces invalid output. Test edge cases explicitly.

Think of quantization like compressing a high-resolution photograph to a lower-resolution thumbnail. The thumbnail captures the important features—shape, color, composition—with a fraction of the storage, but fine detail is lost. Whether that loss matters depends entirely on what you need to do with the image. For most tasks, the thumbnail is sufficient; for tasks requiring fine detail, the original is necessary.

The scale and zero point are the compression codec's parameters—they define how the continuous range of original values maps onto the limited grid of integer values. A poor choice of scale either wastes grid points in a rarely-used range (clipping common values) or crams too many original values into a single integer bucket (losing the ability to distinguish nearby values).

Group quantization is like using a different codec parameter for each region of the image: dense or high-contrast regions get better fidelity because their local statistics are different from flat background regions. This is why per-group quantization consistently outperforms per-tensor quantization for the same total bit budget.
Quantization reduces model weight precision (e.g. FP32 to INT8 or INT4), enabling 4x–8x memory savings and enabling large models to run on consumer hardware.
INT8 quantization typically loses less than 1 % accuracy versus FP16; Q4_K_M (4-bit) loses about 1–3 % on most tasks while fitting very large models on modest hardware.

Post-training quantization (PTQ) is fast to apply and requires no retraining—only calibration data; quantization-aware training (QAT) achieves better quality at aggressive bit widths but requires a training run.
via bitsandbytes are the most practical starting points, covering CPU-only and GPU workflows respectively.
Size up, then quantize: a larger model at Q4 often beats a smaller model at FP16 on difficult tasks, for the same or lower memory cost.
Benchmark quantized models on the actual production task, not just general benchmarks—quantization affects different capabilities unevenly.
Memory bandwidth is the dominant bottleneck for single-batch local inference; quantization reduces bytes-per-parameter and directly reduces time-to-first-token and generation latency.
